exhibits.about: Ranks stocks on how far one data feed sits above its own 120-day average, betting the most stretched will fall back.
Buys stocks whose price swings have widened over the past two weeks and sells those that are calming down.

exhibits.claim: Flight-to-safety nonlinearity (Adrian-Crump-Vogt JF 2019): 1m forward stock returns RISE with lagged VIX while Treasury returns FALL — replication on SPY/TLT 2012-2026 with post-publication subsample
exhibits.reason: Directionally replicates ACV in AND out of sample — monotone SPY rise, TLT mirror — but t=2.25 sits under the 2.5 bar with overlapping-window inference, and far under the 5.0 stressed promotion bar. Verdict: the paper's shape is visible in free data; our sample (2012+) lacks the power their 1990+ sample and sieve estimator had. Graded promising-but-unproven, not vaulted.
exhibits.about: Tests whether scary markets pay stock holders extra while bond prices have already flown to safety — fear as a priced premium, not just a mood.

exhibits.claim: Vol-managed SPY (Moreira-Muir 2017): scale exposure by inverse realized variance, cap 2x — higher Sharpe than buy-and-hold
exhibits.reason: gate t=1.55; promotion needs t>=5.0 (2.5 base + margin-of-safety)
exhibits.about: Holds more stock when markets are calm and less when they are stormy, betting risk isn't paid extra in high-vol months.

exhibits.claim: Betting-against-beta (Frazzini-Pedersen 2014): leverage-constrained investors overpay for high beta; L/S beta-neutral spread earns a premium on our panel
exhibits.reason: gate t=0.29; promotion needs t>=5.0 (2.5 base + margin-of-safety)
exhibits.about: Buys boring low-beta stocks and shorts exciting high-beta ones, levering the boring side to match risk.

exhibits.claim: Turn-of-month effect (McConnell-Xu 2008): SPY returns concentrate in the last 2 + first 3 trading days of the month (mechanical month-end flows)
exhibits.reason: gate t=0.64; promotion needs t>=5.0 (2.5 base + margin-of-safety)
exhibits.about: Owns stocks only around month-end, betting paycheck and fund flows push prices up on a calendar schedule.

exhibits.claim: A 10-20% time-series-momentum sleeve improves the GEOMETRIC growth and max drawdown of an equity book vs equity-only (crisis-alpha portfolio construction; Moskowitz-Ooi-Pedersen lineage). Distinct from the rejected standalone walk-forward TSMOM family: this claims drag reduction at the PORTFOLIO level, not timing alpha
exhibits.reason: 80/20 trend sleeve: CAGR diff -0.06%/yr, t=-0.10 vs bar 2.74 + stressed 2.5 — did not clear
exhibits.about: Adds a small trend-following sleeve to a stock portfolio, betting the hedge cheapens crashes enough to speed up long-run compounding.

exhibits.claim: Tax-loss harvesting adds ~1%/yr after-tax alpha in early years on a direct-indexed S&P portfolio, decaying as embedded gains age (Chaudhuri-Burnham-Lo 2020 replication; the Parametric mechanism)
exhibits.reason: full-liquidation tax alpha -1.75%/yr, t=-2.34: the pre-tax bleed of sell-the-losers swaps (~180bps/yr) dwarfs the tax credits (~6bps/yr on a no-contribution book). Two honest caveats: (1) survivorship interaction — on a basket of today's S&P survivors every deep loser recovers by construction, so a rule that exits drawdowns is structurally punished; the measured bleed is an upper bound. (2) single-name correlation replacement is crude vs a tracking-error optimizer — the implementation IS the product (the Parametric moat). Mechanism not retestable without point-in-time constituents + contribution flows.
exhibits.about: Holds the index as individual stocks and systematically sells losers to bank tax losses, swapping into similar names to keep tracking the index.
Buys big companies whose reported derivative liabilities sit unusually high relative to their own past year.
Buys big stocks whose options now price in much more turbulence than three months ago, and sells those pricing in less.
Bets against stocks where an analyst-estimate figure sits unusually high versus its own past year, and buys the lows.

exhibits.claim: Quantile-regression VaR on SPY from free implied-moment proxies (VIX level, VIX/VIX3M term slope, CBOE SKEW) beats GARCH-based and historical-simulation VaR on coverage and pinball loss (Blom/de Lange/Risstad JRFM 2023, EURUSD -> US equity translation)
exhibits.about: Tests whether free market-fear gauges forecast worst-case daily stock losses better than standard statistical models.

exhibits.claim: GEX states condition the TAILS, not the mean: adding dealer-positioning state to the implied-moment quantile regression improves tail coverage where the Chain's mean forecast failed (chain-family lineage declared for the deflator; per-state residue s2/s6 from v3 is the motivating anomaly, mechanism = short-gamma amplification is a tail phenomenon)
exhibits.about: Tests whether knowing option dealers' positioning sharpens forecasts of the market's worst days, not its typical ones.

exhibits.claim: Dimopoulos Chain v3: shrunk state corrections (GEX x RV cells + term structure + arrival) on a log HAR+VIX+GJR backbone improve 5-day range forecasts (pre-registered spec)
exhibits.about: Tests whether option-dealer positioning and market state improve five-day forecasts of how far the S&P 500 will swing.

exhibits.claim: Dimopoulos Chain v1: GEX x RV state chain adds next-day range information beyond VIX (OOS, monthly-refit)
exhibits.reason: margin-of-safety rerun (edge halved vs VIX baseline, original noise) t=2.17 < 2.5 — edge has no buffer; gate tightened 2026-07-12 (Graham overlay), prior pass was under the old regime
exhibits.about: Tests whether dealer hedging plus recent choppiness predicts tomorrow's trading range beyond the market's fear gauge.
Within industry peer groups, favors stocks whose recent price swings run high relative to an analyst-estimate figure.
Bets against stocks where one company-accounts field sits unusually high versus its own past year, within each sector.
Ranks industry peers on a cryptic price-and-volume data field versus its own six-month average and buys the highs.
Bets against companies with an unusually large gap between shares that exist and shares that could be created.
Buys stocks where an analyst dividend-related count runs high relative to its own six-month average.
Buys stocks whose day-to-day price swings have grown the most over the past three months.
Buys stocks whose option prices tilt unusually far between downside and upside bets compared with the past year.
Bets against companies spending unusually heavily on buying back their own shares compared with their past year.
Buys stocks where a cryptic analyst-estimate field runs unusually high versus its own past year, within each industry.
Buys stocks whose forecast book value per share is unusually high versus its own past year, judged against close peers.
Buys stocks whose average forecast for capital spending rose most over ten days compared with close industry peers.
Ranks companies on the five-day change in employee stock grants and bets against those handing out more.
Bets against companies whose recent cash raised from selling new shares is unusually high versus industry peers.

exhibits.claim: Heston neural forward-approximator: params->IV surface to <0.5 vol pt with >=100x speedup (Horvath-Muguruza-Tomas program)
exhibits.about: Trains a neural network to imitate a slow options-pricing model, trading a long calculation for an instant lookup.

exhibits.claim: Dimopoulos Chain v1.5: neural emissions (MLP on HAR+VIX+GJR+state components) beat the full linear baseline OOS
exhibits.reason: neural emissions did not beat linear ladder (t=-4.37)
exhibits.about: Trains a small neural network on hedging and volatility signals to forecast the size of tomorrow's market moves.

exhibits.claim: VRP timing E_no_steamroller: signal-gated SVXY beats always-long SVXY
exhibits.reason: no significant excess over always-long (t=-0.36, p=0.660)
exhibits.about: Bets on market calm but steps aside whenever warning signs point to a sudden violent snap-back in fear.

exhibits.claim: VRP timing B_contango: signal-gated SVXY beats always-long SVXY
exhibits.reason: no significant excess over always-long (t=0.57, p=0.313)
exhibits.about: Bets on market calm, but only when insurance against swings costs more for later dates than for the near term.

exhibits.claim: VRP timing C_contango_pinned: signal-gated SVXY beats always-long SVXY
exhibits.reason: no significant excess over always-long (t=-1.72, p=0.962)
exhibits.about: Bets on market calm only when swing insurance is pricier further out and dealers' hedging is steadying the market.

exhibits.claim: VRP timing D_pinned: signal-gated SVXY beats always-long SVXY
exhibits.reason: no significant excess over always-long (t=-1.68, p=0.959)
exhibits.about: Bets on market calm only on days when options dealers' hedging tends to pin the market in place.

exhibits.claim: GEX-scaled position sizing (0.5x exposed -> 1.5x pinned) beats plain vol-targeted SPY after costs
exhibits.reason: overlay excess vs plain vol targeting: t=-3.80, p=1.000
exhibits.about: Holds more stock when options dealers' hedging tends to calm the market, and less when it tends to amplify swings.

exhibits.claim: Dimopoulos Chain v1.1: GEX x RV states add next-day range info beyond the combined HAR+VIX+GARCH baseline (OOS, monthly-refit)
exhibits.reason: Passed vs HAR+VIX+plain-GARCH (t_full=2.83) but failed the stricter GJR-augmented baseline (t_full=1.02) in the same-day rerun; duplicate-claim guard prevented overwrite. Demoted 2026-07-10 — the strictest run is the verdict.
exhibits.about: Tests whether dealer-hedging and choppiness states improve on a full battery of standard volatility forecasters.

exhibits.claim: Walk-forward-tuned TSMOM family (216 variants, 5y/1y, DD<-35% filter, max-Sharpe selection) beats B&H out-of-sample
exhibits.reason: stitched OOS excess vs B&H: t=-0.62, p=0.738
exhibits.about: Follows the stock market's trend, re-picking the best-performing trend rule from recent years as it goes.

exhibits.claim: TSMOM vol-targeted SPY (video 'winner' TREND_MOMENTUM_PRO; Moskowitz 2012 lineage) beats buy-and-hold risk-adjusted across regimes
exhibits.reason: excess over B&H is a coin flip (t=0.14, p=0.45); DD reduction real but modest; 9000-attempt source deflator applies
exhibits.about: Tests whether riding the stock market's trend, and easing off when it turns choppy, beats simply holding it.

exhibits.claim: Circular/servitization quality: firms disclosing longer equipment useful lives and larger warranty/service obligations are durable-asset custodians with underpriced earnings quality (Stahel stocks-vs-flows via Rishel/Circudyne)
exhibits.about: Buys companies now reporting longer lifespans for their equipment, betting built-to-last firms are underpriced.

exhibits.claim: Local-news coverage spikes in small caps precede abnormal returns (GDELT volume, v1 all-outlets)
exhibits.about: Tests whether sudden bursts of local news coverage about small companies come before unusual stock moves.
Within each industry, favors companies whose quarterly sales run above their own six-month trend.
Ranks stocks on how one accounting figure compares with its own six-month average and buys the risers.
Within each industry, favors companies whose forecast capital spending is rising versus its recent average.
Within each sector, bets against companies whose yearly sales look unusually high versus their own history.
Favors stocks where an analyst forecast figure runs high compared with its own six-month average.
Ranks stocks by how far an accounting figure sits above its own one-year norm and favors the unusually high ones.
Bets on stocks where a company accounting figure sits unusually high versus its own past year.
Buys stocks whose option markets have grown more fearful of a price fall over the past two weeks.
Buys stocks where analysts' cash-flow forecasts rose over the past two weeks, betting the upgrades keep working.
Bets on stocks whose importance in the customer-supplier network just rose, ranked against industry peers.
Buys stocks whose online chatter runs above its two-month norm, betting spikes in attention move prices.
Tests ranking each stock against close industry peers on how unusual an analyst figure is versus its past year.
Bets against stocks whose option markets have grown more fearful of a price fall over the past month.
Bets against stocks where an analyst estimate figure sits unusually high versus its own six-month history.
Ranks stocks on the 20-day change in a company accounting figure and bets against those where it just rose.
Buys hard-to-trade large stocks whose prices swing on light trading, betting investors earn extra for the hassle.

exhibits.claim: 1-month short-term reversal, retested on the ~500-name S&P universe (Jegadeesh 1990)
exhibits.about: Buys last month's losers and sells its winners, this time only among the 500 largest US stocks.

exhibits.claim: 12-1 momentum, retested on the ~500-name S&P universe (Jegadeesh & Titman 1993)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-0.98 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.798 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.29 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys the past year's winners, skipping the latest month, and sells its losers, only among the 500 largest US stocks.

exhibits.claim: Unusually high recent volume predicts higher returns (Gervais, Kaniel, Mingelgrin 2001)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-2.31 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.987 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.67 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys large stocks trading far more heavily this week than usual, betting the burst of attention pulls in new buyers.

exhibits.claim: Negative return skewness predicts higher returns (skewness preference / lottery overpricing)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-0.87 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.829 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.09 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys large stocks whose surprises tilt toward drops rather than jumps, betting jackpot-style shares are overpriced.

exhibits.claim: Low lottery demand (small max daily gain) predicts higher returns (Bali, Cakici, Whitelaw 2011 MAX effect)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-3.58 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=1.000 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.65 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys large stocks with no big one-day pop in the past month, betting lottery-style shares are overpriced.

exhibits.claim: Low idiosyncratic volatility predicts higher returns (Ang, Hodrick, Xing, Zhang 2006)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-3.89 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=1.000 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.74 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys large stocks with the least stock-specific wobble, betting investors overpay for excitement.

exhibits.claim: Stocks with high trend consistency (share of up days) exhibit continuation — continuous information momentum (Da, Gurun, Warachka 2014 'frog in the pan')
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-1.77 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.948 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.34 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys stocks whose gains come on heavy trading over three months, betting trends confirmed by volume keep going.

exhibits.claim: Proximity to 52-week high predicts returns (George & Hwang 2004)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=-2.22 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.988 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.50 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys stocks sitting near their highest price of the past year, betting hesitant investors let winners keep climbing.

exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=0.58 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.286 > 0.05; IS/OOS degradation 71% > 30% (IS 0.17 -> OOS 0.05) — overfitting signature
exhibits.about: Buys last month's losers and sells its winners, betting crowds overreact and prices snap back.

exhibits.claim: 12-1 month cross-sectional momentum predicts returns (Jegadeesh & Titman 1993)
exhibits.reason: Newey-West t=0.09 < 2.5; bootstrap P(Sharpe<=0)=0.420 > 0.05; in-sample Sharpe -0.02 <= 0
exhibits.about: Buys the past year's biggest gainers, skipping the most recent month, and sells the laggards, betting trends persist.
